Q1: Honestly, my study routine was pretty chaotic on paper, but it worked for me. I'd squeeze in about 20 hours a week, mostly listening to recorded lectures while commuting or doing chores since I learn best through audio. I couldn't always make it to actual classes because of work and other responsibilities, but I'd make up for it by really focusing when I did study. I'd often batch my work around when I had energy and time, which wasn't always predictable.
Q2: My pattern definitely varied a lot based on what was due and what else was going on in my life. With limited resources, I sometimes had to prioritize paid work over studying at regular times. When deadlines approached, I'd go into intense focus mode, but during quieter weeks I might only check in sporadically. I tried to keep some consistency, but life just didn't allow for a perfect schedule.
Q3: Usually it was either a deadline looming or getting a notification about something new posted. Sometimes my peer support group would mention something in our chat and I'd log in to check it out. I wasn't one to browse the LMS for fun—I went in with a purpose, grabbed what I needed, and got out. Occasionally I'd log in just to see if grades were posted because the waiting drove me a bit mad.
Q4: Not really, to be honest. The LMS never became a daily habit for me—it was more of a tool I used when necessary. The peer support intervention helped a bit because my group would remind each other about things, which prompted me to log in more. But it never felt natural or routine; it was always task-driven rather than habitual.
Q5: The recorded lectures were absolutely essential for me since I'm an auditory learner. I'd download them and listen multiple times until concepts clicked. The discussion boards were also helpful because interacting with peers helped clarify things I was confused about. Anything I could listen to rather than read was gold for my learning style.
Q6: I definitely ignored a lot of the reading materials and PDFs because reading just doesn't stick for me the way listening does. Some of the extra resources felt redundant when I'd already understood the concept from lectures. I also skipped over some of the optional quizzes when I was strapped for time—they felt like busywork when I was already confident in the material.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time if it directly connected to what would be assessed or if it genuinely helped me understand something I was struggling with. I didn't have the luxury of time to waste on things that felt like filler. If I could see how something would help my grade or my actual understanding, I was all in. Otherwise, I had to make hard choices about where to spend my limited hours.
Q8: Optional activities felt like a luxury I often couldn't afford. Required ones got done no matter what because I knew they affected my grade. With optional stuff, I'd weigh whether it was actually going to help me or if my time was better spent elsewhere. Sometimes the optional things were genuinely useful, but I had to be strategic about picking which ones to engage with.
Q9: Absolutely, most of my studying happened outside the LMS. I'd download lecture recordings and listen to them on my phone during my commute or while cooking. I also used free YouTube videos and podcasts to supplement what we were learning. My peer group would share notes and explain things to each other over messaging apps, which was incredibly helpful but wouldn't show up anywhere in the system.
Q10: There were so many times I was deeply engaged with course material but the LMS would show nothing. Like when I'd listen to a downloaded lecture three times while working a shift, or when my study group would meet at someone's house and work through problems together. I'd also spend hours just thinking through concepts while doing other tasks—that kind of processing doesn't leave a digital trace.
Q11: All the time, actually. I'd access a lecture video once, download it, and then listen to it repeatedly offline over the next week. Same with any PDFs I actually needed—I'd save them to my phone and review them when I had moments. The initial access was just the starting point; the real learning happened after I'd taken the materials offline.
Q12: There were definitely rough patches, especially when work demanded more hours or when money was tight and stress was high. Sometimes I'd feel bored with the material even though I knew I needed to engage with it—that was frustrating. When multiple deadlines piled up across different areas of my life, the course sometimes had to take a backseat even though I didn't want it to.
Q13: Definitely. When I was exhausted from work or dealing with financial stress, I'd log into the LMS but just couldn't focus properly. Sometimes I'd open materials and realize I was too tired to actually absorb anything. Competing responsibilities were constant—I had to juggle everything carefully, and sometimes the LMS engagement was the thing that got sacrificed even when my learning didn't.
Q14: Yeah, there were times I'd be scrolling through the LMS or have a video playing but my mind was completely elsewhere. I'd be stressed about bills or tired from a long day, and even though I was technically 'accessing' materials, nothing was sinking in. Those sessions felt like going through the motions rather than actually learning.
Q15: I'd sometimes put off longer assignments because they felt overwhelming, especially when I was already stretched thin. Even knowing they were important, I'd tell myself I'd do them when I had more energy or time. It wasn't laziness—it was more like survival mode, where I had to prioritize immediate fires over important-but-not-urgent tasks.
Q16: Deadlines basically dictated when I'd have bursts of LMS activity. I'd cram a lot of engagement into the days right before something was due. It wasn't ideal, but it was how I managed with everything else going on. The deadline pressure actually helped me focus intensely, which is probably why my exam scores were so high despite my attendance being low.
Q17: Feedback definitely motivated me when it was specific and helpful. Good feedback made me want to engage more because I could see my effort paying off. When feedback was delayed or vague, it was harder to stay motivated. My peer support group also gave informal feedback which kept me going even when official feedback was slow.
Q18: The course structure was okay, but it assumed students had more regular availability than I did. The flexibility to access materials anytime was crucial for me—without that, I couldn't have managed. But some of the participation expectations felt designed for students with more predictable schedules and fewer outside pressures.
Q19: Having recorded lectures was a lifesaver for my learning style and schedule. The discussion boards were also good because I could engage asynchronously. What made things harder was when there were live sessions I couldn't attend or when materials were only available in formats that didn't work well for auditory learning. More audio options would have helped a lot.
Q20: I think those students might be searching for what they need, trying to understand the requirements, or maybe struggling to translate their engagement into completed work. Sometimes you can spend hours on the LMS trying to figure things out but still not produce something submittable. It could also be anxiety or perfectionism making it hard to actually submit work.
Q21: That completely reflects my experience. My LMS activity was pretty low, but I got a distinction because my real learning happened offline. I'd download materials, study with peers outside the system, and really engage with content in ways that weren't tracked. Low LMS activity doesn't mean low engagement—it might just mean different engagement.
Q22: Course logs can show when and how often you access things, which gives a surface-level picture of engagement patterns. But they miss the quality of that engagement, the offline studying, the peer discussions, the thinking that happens away from the screen. They definitely can't capture whether someone is actually learning or just clicking through. The numbers tell a very incomplete story.
Q23: To me, being engaged means genuinely wrestling with the material until it makes sense, connecting it to things I already know, and caring about actually understanding rather than just passing. It's about being mentally invested, not just physically present or digitally active. Real engagement is when you find yourself thinking about course concepts even when you're not officially studying.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show how much I relied on my peer support group for motivation and understanding—those conversations happened on WhatsApp and in person. It wouldn't capture the hours I spent listening to lectures while working or commuting. It also can't show the resilience it took to keep pushing despite financial stress and limited resources, or how bored I sometimes felt but pushed through anyway.
